content.problemStatement:
Chef just come up with a very good idea for his business. He needs to hire two group of software engineers. Each group of engineers will work on completely different things and people from different groups don't want to disturb (and even hear) each other. Chef has just rented a whole floor for his purposes in business center "Cooking Plaza". The floor is a rectangle with dimensions **N** over **M** meters. For simplicity of description the floor's structure, let's imagine that it is split into imaginary squares of size **1x1** called "cells".

 The whole floor is split into rooms (not necessarily rectangular). There are some not noise-resistant walls between some of the cells. Two adjacent cells belong to the same room if they don't have the wall between them. Cells are considered adjacent if and only if they share an edge. Also, we say that relation "belong to the same room" is transitive. In other words we say that if cells **A** and **B** belong to the same room and **B** and **C** belong to the same room then **A** and **C** belong to the same room.

So we end up having a partition of the floor into rooms. It also means, that each point on the floor belongs to some room.

Chef have to distribute the rooms between engineers of two groups. Engineers from the different groups cannot seat in the same room. If engineers from a different groups seat in adjacent rooms, the walls these rooms share have to be noise-resistant. The cost of having one meter of wall isolated is **K** per month. Due to various reasons Chef has to pay an additional cost for support of each of the room (e.g. cleaning costs money as well). Interesting to know that support cost for a particular room may differ depending on engineers of which group seat in this room.

Chef doesn't know the number of people he needs in each group of engineers so he wants to minimize the money he needs to pay for all the floor rent and support. He will see how it goes and then redistribute the floor or find another floor to rent or whatever. Either way, you don't need to care about this.

Please pay attention to the restriction that all the rooms should be occupied by engineers of one of the teams. Also, it might happen that all the rooms will be assigned to the same team and this is completely okay.
constraints: All walls are between cells that share an edge.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case the first line contains five integers N, M, W, K and R — the dimensions of the floor, the number of one‑meter walls, the cost K for making a wall noise‑resistant and the number of rooms. The next W lines each contain four integers X1 Y1 X2 Y2 describing a wall between the two adjacent cells (X1,Y1) and (X2,Y2). The next R lines each contain four integers X Y C1 C2 – a cell (X,Y) that belongs to a room and the support cost of that whole room if it is assigned to group 1 (C1) or to group 2 (C2).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with the minimum possible total monthly cost (support costs of all rooms plus K times the total length of walls that separate rooms assigned to different groups).

sampleTestCases.explanation: The correct assignment is the following: the blue color denotes assignment to the first team, and the red color denotes assignment to the second team. Total cost for renting two rooms for the first team is 11 + 10 = 21. Total cost for renting the only room for the second team is 12. There are 3 meters of walls between them, resulting in isolating cost of 15. The grand total is 21 + 12 + 15 = 48.

solution.approach:
The floor can be seen as a grid graph where each cell is a vertex. Walls block the edge between two adjacent cells. First we build the rooms: we run a DSU (union‑find) over all N·M cells and unite two neighboring cells if there is **no** wall between them. After processing the whole grid each DSU set corresponds to one room. The input gives, for each room, a representative cell together with its two possible support costs; we store C1 and C2 for the DSU root of that cell.

Next we need the cost of separating two rooms. Every wall that lies between two different rooms contributes K to the cut if the rooms are assigned to different groups. Therefore we iterate over the list of walls, find the DSU roots of its two cells and, if they differ, increase the weight of the edge (rootA, rootB) by K. After aggregating all walls we obtain a weighted undirected graph whose vertices are the rooms.

Assigning rooms to the two groups with minimum total cost is exactly a minimum s‑t cut problem. We build a flow network:
* source → room i with capacity C1[i] (cost if the room stays on the source side → group 1)
* room i → sink with capacity C2[i] (cost if the room stays on the sink side → group 2)
* for every pair of rooms (u,v) we add two directed edges u→v and v→u each with capacity equal to the total wall cost between them (already multiplied by K).
The value of a minimum s‑t cut equals the minimum possible total cost. We compute it with Dinic's algorithm (O(E·√V) in practice, easily fast for V ≤ 500 and E ≤ R²).

The answer for a test case is the value of the min‑cut.

**Correctness Sketch**
*Union‑Find*: Two cells are in the same DSU set iff there exists a path of adjacent cells without a wall, which is precisely the definition of a room.
*Edge aggregation*: Every wall of length 1 that separates two rooms contributes K to the total cost when the rooms are placed in different groups. Adding K for each such wall to the edge weight reproduces exactly that cost.
*Cut formulation*: In any s‑t cut, a vertex on the source side means the room is assigned to group 1, on the sink side to group 2. Cutting the source→room edge pays C1, cutting the room→sink edge pays C2, and cutting an inter‑room edge pays the accumulated wall cost. Hence the cut cost equals the total cost of the corresponding assignment. Conversely, any assignment defines a cut of the same cost. Therefore the minimum cut gives the optimal assignment.

**Complexities**
*Building DSU*: O(N·M·α(N·M)) ≈ O(N·M). Checking whether a wall exists is O(1) using a hash set.
*Aggregating walls*: O(W·α). At most 150 000 operations.
*Flow*: V = R ≤ 500, E ≤ R² + R (inter‑room edges plus source/sink edges). Dinic runs in O(E·√V) ≈ O(R²·√R) which is well below the limits.
*Memory*: DSU for up to 10⁶ cells, plus the flow graph O(R²). Both fit easily.

**Edge Cases**
*All rooms assigned to the same group – the cut simply does not cut any inter‑room edges.
*No walls (W = 0) – the flow graph has only source/sink edges.
*Multiple walls between the same pair of rooms – they are summed into a single capacity.
*Isolated single‑cell rooms – handled like any other vertex.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N·M + W + R²·√R) per test case
